questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 1.1 Revision on Natural Numbers and Integers
questions.question: A student incorrectly claims that all odd numbers are prime. Which number disproves this claim by being odd but having more than two factors?
questions.hint: Find an odd number that can be written as a product of smaller numbers.
questions.explanation: The claim that all odd numbers are prime is false. 9 is odd but composite (3 × 3). Options A, B, and D are odd primes (exactly two factors).

source_trace.section_anchor: 1.1 Revision on Natural Numbers and Integers
source_trace.content_anchor: composite number has more than two factors

explanations_tiered.definition: A composite number has more than two factors. An odd composite number disproves the false claim that all odd numbers are prime.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: 9 is odd but composite because 9 = 3 × 3, having factors 1, 3, and 9. This shows odd numbers can be non-prime.
explanations_tiered.beginner: 9 is an odd number. You can make 9 as 3 groups of 3, so it's not prime. This shows the claim is wrong.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: 9 is odd and has factors 1, 3, and 9 (more than two), so it is composite. Options 3,7,13 are prime (only factors 1 and themselves).
explanations_tiered.advanced: A counterexample to 'all odd numbers are prime' requires an odd integer n > 1 with a divisor d where 1 < d < n. 9 works (divisor 3).
reasoning_steps: Recall prime definition: exactly two factors.
misconceptions: Some believe 1 is prime (it is not; 1 has only one factor).
The false claim 'all odd numbers are prime' ignores odd composites like 9, 15, 21.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Not all odds are prime. Counterexample: 9 = 3 × 3.

glossary_entry.definition: A natural number greater than 1 with more than two factors.

flashcard.front: Give an odd number that disproves 'all odd numbers are prime'.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What odd number shows that not all odd numbers are prime?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Number with more than two factors.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use this to teach counterexample method in reasoning.
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 1.1 Revision on Natural Numbers and Integers

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 1.1 Revision on Natural Numbers and Integers
questions.question: Using the divisibility test, which number is divisible by 4?
questions.hint: A number is divisible by 4 if its last two digits form a number divisible by 4.
questions.explanation: Divisibility by 4 depends on the last two digits. 5,712 ends with 12, and 12 is divisible by 4. 2,334 ends with 34 (not divisible), 3,186 ends with 86 (not divisible), 42,435 ends with 35 (not divisible).

source_trace.section_anchor: 1.1 Revision on Natural Numbers and Integers
source_trace.structural_element: A number is divisible by 4, if the number formed by its last two digits is divisible by 4
source_trace.content_anchor: 2,334 is not divisible by 4 since last two digits 34 is not divisible by 4

explanations_tiered.definition: A number is divisible by 4 if and only if the two-digit number formed by its last two digits is divisible by 4.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: This rule works because 100 is divisible by 4, so only the last two digits determine divisibility by 4.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Look at the last two digits: 34 (not ÷4), 86 (not), 35 (not), 12 (yes ÷4). So 5,712 works.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Since 100 ≡ 0 (mod 4), any integer n can be written as 100k + r where r is last two digits. n mod 4 = r mod 4. Thus divisibility by 4 depends only on r.
reasoning_steps: Extract last two digits of each number.
Check if that two-digit number is divisible by 4.
Select the number where last two digits ÷ 4 gives integer.
misconceptions: Some divide the entire number by 4 instead of just last two digits.
Confusing with divisibility by 8 rule (last three digits).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Divisible by 4 → last two digits divisible by 4.

glossary_entry.definition: A number is divisible by 4 if its last two digits form a number divisible by 4.

flashcard.front: How can you tell if a number is divisible by 4 without dividing?
flashcard.back: Check if its last two digits form a number divisible by 4.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Which number is divisible by 4: 2,334; 3,186; 42,435; or 5,712?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: 5,712. Last two digits 12 are divisible by 4.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Rule using last two digits to test divisibility by 4.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Check last two digits for ÷4. 12 works → 5,712.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show that 100 is multiple of 4, so hundreds and above don't matter.
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 1.1 Revision on Natural Numbers and Integers

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 1.1 Revision on Natural Numbers and Integers
questions.question: What does the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic state about composite numbers?

options.A: Every composite number has a unique prime factorization
options.B: Every composite number is divisible by 2
options.C: Every composite number can be expressed as a product of two primes
options.D: Every composite number has exactly three factors

questions.hint: This theorem is about uniqueness of prime factorization.
questions.explanation: The Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic states that every composite number can be expressed as a product of primes, and this factorization is unique up to order. Options B, C, and D are false statements about composite numbers.

source_trace.section_anchor: 1.1 Revision on Natural Numbers and Integers
source_trace.content_anchor: Every composite number can be expressed (factored) as a product of primes and this factorization is unique

explanations_tiered.definition: The Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic asserts that every integer greater than 1 is either prime or can be factored uniquely into primes (order aside).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: This theorem guarantees that prime factorization is unique, making primes the 'building blocks' of all natural numbers.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of primes as bricks. Every composite number is made from a unique set of prime bricks, like 12 = 2×2×3 in only one way (order doesn't matter).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Any composite number n > 1 has a prime factorization p₁^a₁ × p₂^a₂ × ... × p_k^a_k, and this representation is unique except for rearranging factors.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Formally: If n = ∏ p_i^{a_i} = ∏ q_j^{b_j} with primes p_i, q_j, then the multisets {p_i^{a_i}} and {q_j^{b_j}} are identical. This underpins number theory.
reasoning_steps: Distinguish from incorrect claims about divisibility or number of factors.
Select option describing unique prime factorization.
misconceptions: Some think factorization is not unique (e.g., 12=3×4, but 4 factors further). Uniqueness means primes only.
Confusing with other theorems like Euclid's lemma.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Fundamental Theorem: Every composite number has a UNIQUE prime factorization.

glossary_entry.definition: Every integer greater than 1 is either prime or can be factored uniquely into prime numbers (order disregarded).

flashcard.front: What does the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic guarantee?
flashcard.back: Unique prime factorization for every composite number.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Every composite number has a unique prime factorization.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize 'unique' — order doesn't matter, but primes do.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: State the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 1.1 Revision on Natural Numbers and Integers
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Options B,C,D are incorrect properties of composites.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 1.1 Revision on Natural Numbers and Integers
questions.subsection: 1.1.5 Greatest common factor and least common multiple
questions.question: Find the greatest common factor (GCF) of 24 and 36.
questions.hint: List factors of each number or use prime factorization.
questions.explanation: Factors of 24: 1,2,3,4,6,8,12,24. Factors of 36: 1,2,3,4,6,9,12,18,36. Common factors: 1,2,3,4,6,12. GCF is 12. Prime factorization: 24=2³×3, 36=2²×3² → common primes: 2²×3=12.

calculation_steps: Take common primes with smallest exponents: \(2^{\min(3,2)} \times 3^{\min(1,2)} = 2^2 \times 3^1\)

source_trace.section_anchor: 1.1 Revision on Natural Numbers and Integers
source_trace.subsection_anchor: 1.1.5 Greatest common factor and least common multiple

explanations_tiered.definition: The greatest common factor (GCF) is the largest positive integer that divides each of the given numbers without remainder.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: GCF can be found by listing factors or using prime factorization: product of common primes to smallest powers.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Prime factorize: 24=2³×3, 36=2²×3². Take smallest exponent of each common prime: 2²×3¹=4×3=12.
worked_solution: Step 4: Smallest exponent for 2: min(3,2)=2; for 3: min(1,2)=1
reasoning_steps: For each common prime, pick the smallest exponent.
misconceptions: Some choose 6 (common factor but not greatest).
Confusing GCF with LCM (LCM would be 72).

glossary_entry.definition: Largest positive integer that divides all given integers.

flashcard.front: What is the GCF of 24 and 36?

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Largest divisor common to all numbers.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Find GCF and LCM of numbers
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 1.1 Revision on Natural Numbers and Integers
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. GCF=12. Options A=6 (common but not greatest), B=8, D=18 are not common divisors.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 1.1 Revision on Natural Numbers and Integers
questions.subsection: 1.1.5 Greatest common factor and least common multiple
questions.question: What is the least common multiple (LCM) of 6 and 9?
questions.hint: List multiples of each number or use prime factorization.
questions.explanation: Multiples of 6: 6,12,18,24,30,36,... Multiples of 9: 9,18,27,36,45,... The smallest common multiple is 18. Prime factors: 6=2×3, 9=3² → LCM = 2¹×3²=18.

calculation_steps: Take highest exponent for each prime: 2¹ (from 6) and 3² (from 9)

source_trace.section_anchor: 1.1 Revision on Natural Numbers and Integers
source_trace.subsection_anchor: 1.1.5 Greatest common factor and least common multiple

explanations_tiered.definition: The least common multiple (LCM) is the smallest positive integer that is a multiple of all given numbers.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: LCM can be found by listing multiples or using prime factorization: product of highest powers of all primes appearing.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Multiples of 6: 6,12,18,24... Multiples of 9: 9,18,27... First match is 18.
reasoning_steps: For each prime, take the largest exponent present.
misconceptions: Some select 3 (GCF, not LCM).
Choosing 36 (common multiple but not least).

glossary_entry.definition: Smallest positive integer that is a multiple of all given numbers.

publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Find GCF and LCM of numbers
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 1.1 Revision on Natural Numbers and Integers
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. LCM=18. Option A=3 (GCF), C=36 (multiple but not least), D=54.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.subsection: 1.2.2 Conversion of repeating decimals into fractions
questions.structural_anchor: Conversion of repeating decimals into fractions
questions.question: Convert the repeating decimal \(0.\overline{5}\) (0.555...) into a fraction in simplest form.
questions.hint: Let d = 0.555..., multiply by 10, subtract, then solve for d.
questions.explanation: Let \(d = 0.\overline{5}\). Then \(10d = 5.\overline{5}\). Subtract: \(10d - d = 5.\overline{5} - 0.\overline{5}\) → \(9d = 5\) → \(d = \frac{5}{9}\). Option A is 0.5 (terminating), C is 0.5, D would be 0.050505...

source_trace.subsection_anchor: 1.2.2 Conversion of repeating decimals into fractions

explanations_tiered.definition: A repeating decimal can be converted to a fraction by setting it equal to a variable, multiplying by a power of 10 to align repeating parts, and subtracting.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For a single repeating digit, multiply by 10, subtract original equation, solve for variable.
reasoning_steps: Subtract original to eliminate repeating part.
misconceptions: Some incorrectly write 5/10 = 0.5, forgetting the repeating bar.
Using denominator 99 for single repeating digit (should be 9).

glossary_entry.definition: Algebraic method converting a repeating decimal to a rational fraction.

flashcard.front: Convert 0.555... (0.5 with bar) to a fraction.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show geometric series method as alternative.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Simplify the expression \(\sqrt{8} + \sqrt{2}\).
questions.hint: Simplify \(\sqrt{8}\) to \(2\sqrt{2}\) first, then add like radicals.
questions.explanation: \(\sqrt{8} = \sqrt{4 \times 2} = 2\sqrt{2}\). Then \(2\sqrt{2} + \sqrt{2} = (2+1)\sqrt{2} = 3\sqrt{2}\). Option A incorrectly adds radicands; C and D are not equivalent.

explanations_tiered.definition: Like radicals (same index and radicand) can be added by adding coefficients.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Simplify each radical separately, then combine like terms.
misconceptions: Adding radicands directly: √8+√2 ≠ √10.
Forgetting to simplify √8 first.

glossary_entry.definition: Radicals with same index and same radicand that can be combined by adding coefficients.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize simplifying each radical before combining.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. √8=2√2, sum=3√2. Option A (√10) is incorrect addition of radicands.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Calculate \(\sqrt{2} \times \sqrt{3}\).
questions.explanation: The product of square roots equals the square root of the product: \(\sqrt{2} \times \sqrt{3} = \sqrt{2 \times 3} = \sqrt{6}\). Option A adds radicands; C incorrectly multiplies; D incorrectly places coefficient.

explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Multiplication of square roots combines radicands under a single radical.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Multiply inside the square roots: 2×3=6, so √2 × √3 = √6.
worked_solution: Step 1: Recall product rule for radicals: √a × √b = √(ab)
misconceptions: Adding radicands: √2×√3 ≠ √5.
Forgetting the radical: √2×√3 ≠ 6.

publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. √2×√3=√6. Option A adds radicands, C removes radical, D incorrect coefficient.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Which interval notation represents all real numbers between -3 and 8, including both endpoints?
questions.hint: Square brackets [ ] indicate inclusion of endpoints.
questions.explanation: In interval notation, square brackets [ ] mean the endpoint is included. Parentheses ( ) mean excluded. Including both -3 and 8 requires [-3, 8]. Option A excludes both; C excludes 8; D excludes -3.

explanations_tiered.definition: A closed interval [a,b] includes all real numbers x such that a ≤ x ≤ b.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Bracket [ means include endpoint; parenthesis ( means exclude endpoint.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Square brackets [ ] mean 'include this number'. So both -3 and 8 included → [-3,8].
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Including endpoints requires square brackets at both ends: [-3,8] means -3 ≤ x ≤ 8.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Closed intervals are compact sets in ℝ; [a,b] contains its endpoints a and b, unlike open intervals.
misconceptions: Confusing parentheses and brackets: (a,b) excludes both endpoints.
Writing intervals in reverse order: must be [lower,upper].

flashcard.front: How do you write all real numbers between -3 and 8 inclusive?

visual_system.visual_description: Number line from -4 to 9. Filled circles at -3 and 8. Thick line segment connecting them shading all points between.
visual_system.caption: Figure: Closed interval [-3,8] includes both endpoints.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Number line with filled circles at -3 and 8, line between them shaded.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is interval notation for x such that -3 ≤ x ≤ 8?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use number line visual to reinforce inclusion/exclusion.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Use interval notation to represent sets of real numbers
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Option A excludes both, C excludes 8, D excludes -3.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Find the distance between -3 and 4 on the number line.
questions.explanation: Distance = |(-3) - 4| = |-7| = 7. Also |4 - (-3)| = |7| = 7. Distance is always non-negative, so options A, C, D are incorrect.

explanations_tiered.definition: Distance between points a and b on the number line is |a - b|, the absolute value of their difference.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Absolute value gives non-negative distance. Compute difference, then remove negative sign.
misconceptions: Subtracting in wrong order without absolute value gives negative distance.
Counting only whole units but forgetting -3 to 4 includes 7 steps.

glossary_entry.definition: Absolute difference between two real numbers.

flashcard.front: What is the distance between -3 and 4?

visual_system.visual_description: Number line from -4 to 5. Points -3 and 4 are marked. Double-headed arrow between them labeled '7 units'.
visual_system.caption: Figure: Distance of 7 units between -3 and 4.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Number line showing distance 7 from -3 to 4.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Remind students distance is always non-negative.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Find distance between real numbers using absolute value
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Distance = 7. Options A=1 (difference without sign?), C negative, D negative.
